glossary.def: The fixed wrapper every API response uses: { schema, generated_at, dataset_version, recently_updated_days, data }. The payload always lives under data, so consumers parse one stable shape regardless of endpoint.
A SHA-256 content hash of the dataset. It is written to KV only when the content changes, and it doubles as the ETag on every read route.

The single shape /v1/locations serves: every field on every entry (identity, coordinates, tags, description, credits, image URLs, recently_updated). There is no slim variant; ?full=1 is accepted as a no-op alias for older consumers.

A server-computed boolean on every location record: true when the mod's Nexus update falls within the recency window. The server computes it because the in-game consumer has no clock to compute it with.
The number of days behind recently_updated, published on every envelope as recently_updated_days (currently 7). Consumers read the rule from the envelope instead of hardcoding it.

The install files a mod ships, published on every location record: its .archive load files AND .xl (ArchiveXL) files, both of which land in the game's archive/pc/mod/ folder and are readable by another mod (.xl is the only fingerprint a removal-only mod has). Bare filenames, not paths. An in-game consumer matches them against a player's installed files to tell which location mods are present. Empty when a mod's files cannot be read (a WIP/Dummy entry with no Nexus page, an AMM-only mod, or not yet fetched), never 'ships nothing'.
A location's permanent identifier: a UUID for manual entries, or nexus-<nexus_id> for auto-discovered ones. Frozen by contract so consumers can key on it.

The in-game Cyberpunk JSON reader the API must stay mappable to. It turns a response into a DTO (Data Transfer Object, a plain data record with named fields), and it is strict: no arrays-of-arrays, and property names are case-sensitive. This constraint shapes the whole contract.
Application Programming Interface: how one program asks another for data through software, instead of a person using a screen.
A specific address on an API you can call, such as /v1/locations. Each endpoint returns one kind of data.
JavaScript Object Notation: a plain-text data format of names and values (objects and arrays) that most languages, browsers, and the game can read.
HyperText Transfer Protocol: the request-and-response language a client and server use to exchange data on the web.
A number in an HTTP response saying how it went: 200 OK, 304 Not Modified, 404 Not Found, 503 not ready.
An HTTP response header that is an identifier for a specific version of a resource. Sent back by the client as If-None-Match to ask 'has this changed?'.
A request carrying a validator (like If-None-Match) so the server can reply 304 Not Modified instead of resending an unchanged body.
A status code telling the client there is no need to retransmit the resource; its cached copy is still valid. The response carries no body.
A Cache-Control directive: the cache may reuse a stale response while it revalidates in the background, so clients never block on a refresh.
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing: an HTTP-header mechanism that lets a server declare which other origins a browser may load its resources from.
A serverless function running on Cloudflare's global edge network, with no infrastructure to manage. Handles each request in its fetch() handler.
A global, low-latency key-value data store optimized for high reads and infrequent writes. The API serves the whole dataset from KV.
A Cloudflare feature that maps a cron expression to a Worker's scheduled() handler so it runs on a schedule.
A flag on /v1/meta. true means the last refresh failed and you are being served the last-known-good data; false means the data is fresh.
The safety rule: if a refresh fails, keep serving the last dataset that built cleanly rather than an empty or partial one.
The rule that the refresh saves new data only when its content fingerprint (hash) has changed, so unchanged data keeps a stable dataset_version and ETag.

A liveness stamp the refresh job writes on EVERY cron cycle (last_refresh_at), served at /v1/health with a server-computed refresh_age_seconds. Because it moves every cycle, unlike generated_at (which only moves when the content changes), a heartbeat that stops advancing reveals a wedged cron that is still serving but silently going stale, a failure discovery_stale cannot catch. refresh_age_seconds also gives the clockless in-game consumer a freshness read.
The Cloudflare account identifier, set in wrangler.jsonc. Not secret; setting it lets the deploy's scoped token skip an account-lookup step.

SemVer for the API SURFACE, set by hand in wrangler.jsonc and reported at /v1/health as data.version. MINOR when a field or route is added, MAJOR on a breaking change (which also moves /v1 to /v2), PATCH on a behavior fix. It does double duty: proof that a given deploy landed, and the signal that tells a consumer whether the field it wants exists yet. It is not the site's release version, not dataset_version (which tracks the data, not the shape), and not the in-game ApiVersion() integer (which gates only on breaking changes, so the two numbers will not match). It stood at 0.1.0 across three additive changes until nc-zoning-board 1.7.0 backfilled it to 1.3.0 and added a CI guard.
A status code meaning the server is not ready to handle the request (a temporary condition). Here it is the not_ready response before the first dataset build, and it carries Retry-After.
A response header saying how long to wait before retrying, given as seconds or a date. Used with 503 and 429.
Capping how many requests a client may send in a time window; here 100 per 10 seconds per IP, enforced at Cloudflare's edge.
A status code meaning you sent too many requests in a given time: slow down and retry. From the edge it carries no JSON envelope.
Web Application Firewall: Cloudflare's edge security layer, where the API's rate-limiting rule lives.
A free Cloudflare feature that challenges suspected automated traffic. Disabled for this zone because it challenged the project's own CI health monitor.
Cloudflare's servers around the world that sit in front of the Worker; caching and security rules act here, before your code runs.
Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment: automation that builds, tests, and ships the project on each push, with no manual deploy steps.

glossary.def: A credential scoped to only the permissions it needs (least privilege), used by the deploy to prove it may act on Cloudflare.
A standard, language-neutral format for describing an HTTP API so people and machines can understand it without reading the code. This API's description lives at /openapi.json.
A test that fails CI if the OpenAPI spec and the routes the Worker actually serves disagree in either direction (undocumented route, or a documented path that is no longer served).
An API-documentation renderer. The API root page (GET /) uses it to turn openapi.json into browsable, interactive docs.
Any program that calls the API. Here there are two: the in-game mod (NCZoningCore) and the website. Both read the same /v1/locations records and derive what they need locally.

The website's failure posture: it has NO client-side fallback. If the Data API call fails in any way (network, non-2xx, malformed, or a slim payload), the site throws and shows a loud 'map data unavailable' state instead of a silent empty map. That makes the public site a canary for API outages, because the API's other consumer, in-game mods, has no fallback either.

glossary.def: The packed identifier Nexus needs for images: (gameId << 32) + modId as a single number, NOT 'gameId:modId'. A wrong format makes Nexus silently return no images.

The in-game mod's compatibility contract: ApiVersion() returns an integer that only increments on a breaking change, so a dependent mod can gate on it at runtime (if ApiVersion() < 1 return). redscript cannot otherwise check a dependency's version.
NCZoningCore parses network responses off the game thread and marshals results back onto the game thread before firing any signal, so every callback and read a consumer sees runs on the game thread.
