modules.title: TRANSMISSION PROTOCOL
modules.subtitle: The API contract: envelope, stable ids, one record shape

lines: > subject: the shape of every message the API sends
> note: the seal (schema / generated_at / dataset_version / recently_updated_days) never changes.
> only what sits under `data` changes per route. begin.

objectives: Name the five fields of the response envelope and where the payload lives
Describe the one location record shape every route serves, including the server-computed recently_updated flag
Explain why path versioning (/v1) and stable ids keep consumers from breaking

chunks.heading: Field manual: how to read a data API
chunks.body: Before the contract, the vocabulary. An **API** (Application Programming Interface) is how one program asks another for data, instead of a person clicking a screen. This project's API lives at `https://api.nczoning.net`. A program sends it an HTTP **request** for a particular address (an **endpoint**, like `/v1/locations`) and gets back a **response**. **HTTP** (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is simply the language of that request-and-response conversation. Each request names a **method** (here always `GET`, meaning 'fetch this'), and each response has three parts: a **status code** (`200` means OK, `404` means not found), some **headers** (labels that describe the response), and a **body** (the data itself). That body is **JSON** (JavaScript Object Notation), a plain-text format of names and values that a web browser and the game can both read. Every bold term here returns throughout the course, so keep this page handy.

chunks.heading: One envelope, every response
chunks.body: Every response is a JSON object wrapped in the **same five-field envelope**: `schema`, `generated_at`, `dataset_version`, `recently_updated_days`, and `data`. The part you asked for (the **payload**) always sits under `data`, so a program reads one predictable shape whether it requested a location, a district, or even an error. (`recently_updated_days` publishes the recency window; module 02 shows what it drives.) Why fix the shape? Because the Cyberpunk game itself is a consumer, and its built-in JSON reader (called **RedData**) maps each response onto a **DTO** (Data Transfer Object, a plain data record with named fields). That reader is strict: no arrays inside arrays, and field names are case-sensitive (so `nexus_id` is not the same as `Nexus_Id`). The envelope keeps every response friendly to that reader.

chunks.heading: The envelope, live
chunks.body:
GET /v1/meta HTTP/1.1
Host: api.nczoning.net

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Cache-Control: public, max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=3600
ETag: "cc90d381ee82cde1bc1229aa4d8cccdd219826587261041249245b9405d36912"

{
  "schema": 1,
  "generated_at": "2026-07-21T08:05:06.803Z",
  "dataset_version": "cc90d381ee82cde1bc1229aa4d8cccdd219826587261041249245b9405d36912",
  "recently_updated_days": 7,
  "data": { "discovery_stale": false, "skipped": [ { "nexus_id": "31383", "name": "watson warehouse apartment and garage" } ] }
}

chunks.heading: One record, every field
rows: Always present; id is the stable anchor (see below)
district is never null (outside every polygon defaults to Badlands); subdistrict may be null
credits appears only when set; the image fields are null when unknown (WIP/Dummy entries)
Server-computed: true when updated_at falls within the envelope's recently_updated_days
The .archive and .xl files the mod ships, so an in-game mod can match them against a player's archive/pc/mod/ folder; empty when unknowable
chunks.body: A **representation** is just the shape of the data you get back, and this API has exactly **one**: every entry on `/v1/locations` carries every field. (`?full=1` in the address is accepted as a harmless no-op alias, kept so older consumers that used to ask for a 'full' variant keep working.) There are no precomputed statistics anywhere: a consumer that wants per-district counts or a category breakdown **derives them by grouping these records**: every consumer holds the whole registry anyway, and one source of truth cannot disagree with itself.

chunks.heading: Frozen: ids and the /v1 prefix
chunks.body: Two things never change under you. **Stable ids**: manual entries keep their **UUID** (Universally Unique Identifier, a long one-off random string), and auto-discovered ones use `nexus-<nexus_id>`. Either way, the id is safe to store and look up by. **Path versioning**: every endpoint lives under `/v1/`, changes inside it are *additive* (new fields appear; fields something reads are never removed or renamed), and a breaking change would ship as a new `/v2/`, never as a silent change to `/v1/`. One honest asterisk on 'additive': in 1.4.0 the maintainers **removed** `meta.counts` from `/v1` without a version bump - but only after auditing every consumer (the website discarded `/meta`, the in-game mod derives counts from records, and both monitors read only the health flags) and proving nothing read it. That is the real-world rule: the contract protects **consumers**, not field names. A field something reads is frozen; a field nothing reads is dead code wearing a contract's uniform, and it can be buried once you have *verified* - not assumed - that no one attends the funeral.

lab.title: MANIFEST INSPECTION
lab.briefing: Pull the location manifest and read a record end to end. Then flip the legacy full flag and watch NOTHING change - one representation, one ETag.
steps: TRANSMIT the default request and read the first entry under data[]
Find the five envelope fields, then the record's recently_updated flag
Set the full flag to 1 and TRANSMIT again
Compare the two responses: same body, same ETag - the flag is a no-op alias

shapeHints: data is an array of full records
full=1 changes nothing: same body, same ETag

data.name: 402 north oak - brutalist apartment house home
data.description: brutalist home in the north oak hills with a view of the city from the flowing infinite pool

data.name: 402 north oak - brutalist apartment house home
data.description: brutalist home in the north oak hills with a view of the city from the flowing infinite pool

lab.debrief: The two responses are byte-for-byte identical: one representation, one ETag, and ?full=1 is a no-op alias kept for older consumers. In the previous API the flag selected a bigger body with its own -full ETag; that split is gone, which is one less way for a cache to hand you the wrong shape. Real captures truncated here to the first of 296 entries.

quiz.prompt: A consumer wants to detect when the dataset has changed. Which envelope field should it watch?

options.text: dataset_version
options.feedback: Correct. It is the content hash and doubles as the ETag; a new value means the data changed.

options.text: generated_at
options.feedback: Close, but generated_at is a timestamp of the build - it can move without the data meaningfully changing. dataset_version is the content hash.

options.text: schema
options.feedback: No. schema is the envelope format version (currently 1); it changes almost never.

options.text: data
options.feedback: That is the payload itself; diffing it every time is exactly what dataset_version lets you avoid.

quiz.prompt: Which statements about the location record and its recency flag are TRUE? Select all that apply.

options.text: Every record carries every field - there is no slim variant to opt out of.
options.feedback: Yes. One representation; ?full=1 is only a no-op alias for older consumers.

options.text: recently_updated is computed by the server, not the client.
options.feedback: Yes - and module 02 explains why: the in-game consumer has no clock to compute it with.

options.text: The recency window (in days) is published on the envelope as recently_updated_days.
options.feedback: Yes. Consumers read the rule from the envelope instead of hardcoding '7 days'.

options.text: The API also serves per-district counts, so consumers never have to count anything.
options.feedback: No - the opposite. The API ships per-location records ONLY; every consumer derives counts by grouping the records it already holds.

options.text: district can be null for locations outside every district polygon.
options.feedback: No - district is never null; anywhere outside every polygon is Badlands, the game's own default.

quiz.prompt: A new consumer is being written against /v1. Which assumption is UNSAFE?

options.text: "I can rely on the array order of data[] to identify entries by position."
options.feedback: This is the unsafe one. The list is sorted by name and entries come and go; key on the stable id, never on position.

options.text: "An entry's id will not change between fetches."
options.feedback: Safe. Ids are frozen by contract (UUID or nexus-<id>).

options.text: "Every response will have schema, generated_at, dataset_version and data."
options.feedback: Safe. That is the envelope guarantee, on every route including errors.

options.text: "New fields may be added inside /v1 without a version bump."
options.feedback: Safe. /v1 changes are additive; a breaking change would be /v2.

scenario.title: FIELD REPORT // THE HARDCODED PIN
situation: > support ticket from a mod author consuming the API
> 'my in-game marker used to point at the Afterlife bar. now it points at some apartment.'
> their code: fetch /v1/locations, then take data[3] as 'the Afterlife entry'
> overnight, three new mods were auto-discovered; the list re-sorted by name
> data[3] is now a completely different location

question.prompt: What should the author have keyed on instead of the array position data[3]?

options.text: The entry's stable id (its UUID or nexus-<id>), which never changes.
options.feedback: Correct. The list is sorted by name and grows as mods are added, so position is not stable. The id is frozen by contract: find the entry by id and it is always the Afterlife.

options.text: The array position, but re-checked on every app launch.
options.feedback: Positions shift whenever a mod is added, removed, or renamed. Re-reading a fragile anchor does not make it stable.

options.text: The entry's name string, matched exactly.
options.feedback: Better than position, but authors can edit names and they are not guaranteed unique. The id is the contract's anchor.

scenario.debrief: What the contract guarantees: every entry has a stable id (a UUID for manual mods, nexus-<nexus_id> for auto-discovered ones), and the list is sorted by name, so it changes as mods come and go. data[3] is a moving target; the id is not. The rule: key on the id, never on array position. (This is a constructed scenario, but the sort-by-name and the id guarantee it hinges on are both real, in the code below.)

recap: Every response uses the same five-field envelope; the payload is always under data.
/v1/locations serves one record shape with every field, including the server-computed recently_updated and the archives install-detection list; consumers derive their own counts by grouping records.
Ids are frozen (UUID or nexus-<id>) and /v1 is versioned by path - key on ids, not positions.

modules.title: FRESHNESS DIRECTIVE
modules.subtitle: HTTP caching, ETags, 304s and stale-while-revalidate

lines: > anomaly report: 'client keeps re-downloading 300 KB of unchanged data'
> anomaly report: 'another client shows district data that looks stale'
> directive: learn the freshness handshake - ETag, If-None-Match, 304

objectives: Trace a conditional GET end to end and produce a real 304
Explain why the ETag is the dataset content hash
Read Cache-Control: max-age plus stale-while-revalidate and say what each does

chunks.heading: The ETag is the content hash
chunks.body: An **ETag** (entity tag) is, in MDN's words, 'an identifier for a specific version of a resource'. Here it is not arbitrary: the ETag *is* the `dataset_version`, which is a **SHA-256 hash** of the dataset (a short fingerprint of the content: identical content always produces the same fingerprint, and any change produces a different one). The background job that rebuilds the data saves a new copy only when that fingerprint changes, so a stable ETag genuinely means 'nothing changed'. On a read, the API compares the `If-None-Match` value you send against the current ETag before it ever reads the large payload.

chunks.heading: The conditional handshake
chunks.body:
# First fetch: you get the ETag
GET /v1/meta HTTP/1.1

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
ETag: "cc90d381...5d36912"

# Next fetch: hand the ETag back
GET /v1/meta HTTP/1.1
If-None-Match: "cc90d381...5d36912"

HTTP/1.1 304 Not Modified
ETag: "cc90d381...5d36912"
Cache-Control: public, max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=3600
(no body)

chunks.heading: The clock can change the ETag
chunks.body: Here is a subtlety the recency flag forces. `recently_updated` depends on **today's date**: a mod updated 7 days ago is `true` this morning and `false` tomorrow, with no change on Nexus at all. But the ETag only changes when the *content* hash changes - so if the flag were computed on read, the body could change while the ETag stayed put, and a conditional GET would wrongly answer `304` with a stale flag baked into your cache. The fix: the flag is computed **at rebuild time and folded into the hashed content**, so a mod ageing out of the window genuinely changes `dataset_version` (and your ETag) even though nothing on Nexus moved. The honest side effect: the ETag can now change on a quiet day. That is not noise - it is the recency boundary sweeping past a mod.

sources.label: worker/src/merge.js L123-141 (recency rides the rehashed content)
worker/src/refresh.js L225-237 (the hash the ETag comes from)

chunks.heading: Reading the Cache-Control line
rows: May be stored in a shared cache (a CDN, or Content Delivery Network, or the browser).
Fresh for 300 s after it was generated; after that it is stale.
For up to 3600 s past freshness, a cache may serve the stale copy while it revalidates in the background.
chunks.body: So a client never blocks on a refresh: within the hour after expiry it gets an instant (stale) answer while the cache quietly checks for a new version.

lab.title: CONDITIONAL RETRIEVAL
lab.briefing: TRANSMIT once to capture the ETag, then TRANSMIT again with If-None-Match set to it. A matching ETag returns a bodyless 304.
steps: TRANSMIT with no If-None-Match header - note the ETag in the response
Copy that ETag into the If-None-Match header
TRANSMIT again - read the status line and the (missing) body

shapeHints: a matching If-None-Match yields 304 with no body
the ETag is echoed on both

lab.debrief: The 304 carries the ETag and Cache-Control but no body - exactly what MDN says a 304 must do. The client keeps its cached copy; you moved a few hundred header bytes instead of the whole dataset. This 304 was captured live on 2026-07-22.

quiz.prompt: Put the steps of a conditional GET in order.
steps: Client makes a normal GET and receives a 200 with an ETag
Client stores the ETag alongside the cached body
Later, client repeats the GET with If-None-Match set to the stored ETag
Server compares the ETag to the current dataset_version
If unchanged, server returns 304 with no body; the client reuses its cache

quiz.prompt: The response says stale-while-revalidate=3600. A cache holds a copy that went stale 10 minutes ago. What happens on the next request?

options.text: The cache serves the stale copy immediately and revalidates in the background.
options.feedback: Correct. 10 min is within the 3600 s SWR window, so the client is not blocked while the cache checks for a new version.

options.text: The cache blocks the client until it has fetched a fresh copy.
options.feedback: That is plain expiry behavior. stale-while-revalidate exists precisely to avoid blocking within its window.

options.text: The cache discards the copy and returns a 304.
options.feedback: A cache does not mint 304s, and it would not discard a copy still usable under SWR.

quiz.prompt: Which statement about the 304 response is WRONG?

options.text: "A 304 includes the full resource body so the client can refresh its copy."
options.feedback: This is the wrong one. A 304 must NOT contain a body; the client reuses the body it already cached.

options.text: "A 304 echoes the ETag of the current resource."
options.feedback: True - the captured 304 carries the ETag.

options.text: "A 304 is triggered by If-None-Match matching the current ETag."
options.feedback: True - a matching validator is exactly what produces the 304.

options.text: "A 304 still carries Cache-Control."
options.feedback: True - it sends the headers an equivalent 200 would.

scenario.title: SIMULATED INCIDENT // CACHE-TTL OVERRIDE
situation: > [ TRAINING SCENARIO - constructed exercise, grounded in the real cache config ]
> ticket: 'a mod I just submitted still isn't on the map after 3 minutes'
> observed: /v1/meta ETag unchanged; Cache-Control: max-age=300, stale-while-revalidate=3600
> known: prod dataset cron runs every 5 minutes

question.prompt: Is this an outage, or the system working as designed?

options.text: Working as designed - a new submission can take up to one cron cycle (5 min) to enter the dataset, and a cached copy can be served for up to max-age (5 min) plus the SWR window on top.
options.feedback: Correct. Propagation delay = cron cadence + edge cache freshness, all cited config. 3 minutes is simply inside that window.

options.text: Outage - the ETag should change the instant a mod is submitted.
options.feedback: The ETag only changes when the cron rebuilds and the content hash differs; it is not event-driven per submission.

options.text: Bug - max-age should be 0 so data is always fresh.
options.feedback: max-age=0 would throw away the whole point of the cache and hammer KV. The 5-min windows are a deliberate propagation/cost trade.

scenario.debrief: What the config actually dictates: propagation is bounded by the 5-minute prod cron plus a 300 s max-age (with a 3600 s SWR tail a shared cache may use). A 3-minute wait is expected, not an incident. NOTE: this is a constructed training scenario built from the cited cron + cache values, not a recorded incident - if there is a real cache-TTL episode you want represented here, point me to it and I will swap in the true debrief.

recap: The ETag is the dataset content hash; If-None-Match lets the server answer 304 without resending the body.
A 304 carries headers (ETag, Cache-Control) but no body - cheaper than the full payload.

max-age=300 sets freshness; stale-while-revalidate=3600 serves stale instantly while revalidating; and because recently_updated is folded into the hash, the ETag can flip on a quiet day when a mod ages out of the recency window.

modules.title: THE ENGINE ROOM
modules.subtitle: How the API runs: Cloudflare Workers plus KV

lines: > mission type: FIELD RECON (no incident) - trace the machine, do not fix it
> question: when a request hits api.nczoning.net, what actually answers it?
> follow the request from the edge, through the router, into the store.

objectives: Describe what handles a request: a Cloudflare Worker's fetch() handler and its route table
Explain where the data lives (Workers KV) and why reads are cheap
Read /v1/health, interpret its cron heartbeat (last_refresh_at, refresh_age_seconds), and say why it is no-store with no ETag

chunks.heading: A function at the edge
chunks.body: There is no server for us to run or patch. The API is a **Cloudflare Worker**, which Cloudflare describes as 'a serverless platform for building, deploying, and scaling apps across Cloudflare's global network... no infrastructure to manage'. **Serverless** means we write only the function that answers a request, and Cloudflare runs it for us. It runs at the **edge**: Cloudflare's servers spread around the world, close to whoever is calling. Each request enters the Worker's `fetch()` function, which deals with the browser's **CORS** (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) preflight and method checks, then looks the path up in a small route table (`GET /v1/health`, `GET /v1/locations`, and so on). A path that matches nothing returns a 404 (not found).

chunks.heading: The store is KV
chunks.body: The Worker holds no data itself. It reads everything from **Workers KV**, which Cloudflare describes as 'a global, low-latency, key-value data store'. A **key-value store** is the simplest kind of database: you save a value under a name (its **key**) and read it straight back by that name. The dataset is served from four keys: `dataset:v1:full` (the location records, keyed by id - `/v1/locations` serves its values and `/v1/locations/{id}` reads one entry), `dataset:v1:districts`, `dataset:v1:tags` and `dataset:v1:meta`. (A fifth key, `dataset:v1:archives`, is an internal cross-run cache the rebuild keeps of each mod's install files, not a served route.) A read route just fetches its served key and wraps it in the envelope. That imbalance is the whole design: the rebuild writes occasionally, the world reads constantly.

sources.label: worker/src/store.js L1-31 (KV keys: four served + the archives cache)

chunks.heading: One writer, many readers
chunks.body: The same Worker has two entry points. `fetch()` answers requests (reading from KV). `scheduled()` runs on a **cron trigger**: a schedule, written in the standard cron format, that Cloudflare uses to run the Worker's `scheduled()` function at fixed times. It rebuilds the dataset into KV every 5 minutes in production. Requests and the rebuild never block each other.

chunks.heading: The liveness probe, and the cron heartbeat
chunks.body:
GET /v1/health HTTP/1.1
Host: api.nczoning.net

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Cache-Control: no-store

{ "schema": 1, "generated_at": null, "dataset_version": null, "recently_updated_days": 7, "data": { "status": "ok", "version": "0.1.0", "last_refresh_at": "2026-07-21T22:00:06.803Z", "refresh_age_seconds": 149 } }

# status/version: liveness + deploy marker (data is null: health reads no dataset)
# last_refresh_at + refresh_age_seconds: the cron heartbeat (see below)
# Cache-Control: no-store, so the probe always reads origin, never an edge copy

chunks.heading: The heartbeat catches a cron that stopped
chunks.body: Module 04 introduces `discovery_stale`, which flips to `true` when a refresh RUNS and FAILS. But what if the 5-minute cron stops firing at all? Then no refresh runs, nothing fails, `discovery_stale` stays `false`, and the API keeps serving the last good snapshot: silently stale, every health flag green. That is the gap the heartbeat closes. `last_refresh_at` is stamped on EVERY cron cycle (a content change, a no-change tick, or a failure), so it advances even when `generated_at` (which moves only on a content change) does not. `refresh_age_seconds` is its age. If that age climbs past one cron interval, the cron has wedged, even though `discovery_stale` never noticed. The same number is also a freshness read for the in-game consumer, which has no clock of its own to compute one.

lab.title: SYSTEMS CHECK
lab.briefing: Ping the liveness probe. Read the deployed version and the cron heartbeat, and notice the one cache header it does carry.
steps: TRANSMIT the health request
Read data.version (the deployed API_VERSION) and data.refresh_age_seconds (seconds since the cron last ran)
Check the headers: Cache-Control is no-store and there is no ETag - work out why each is right for a probe

shapeHints: data carries version, last_refresh_at and refresh_age_seconds
Cache-Control: no-store and no ETag - a probe must read live and has no hashed body to tag

lab.debrief: Health returns the envelope with null generated_at and dataset_version because it reads no served dataset - status and version are a pure liveness and deploy check. It is not cacheable content, so it carries no ETag; and it is marked Cache-Control: no-store so a probe always reaches origin, never a stale edge copy. The one thing it DOES read is the small meta record, for the cron heartbeat: last_refresh_at is when the 5-minute refresh cron last ran (stamped every cycle), and refresh_age_seconds is its age. Watch that age: if it climbs past a few minutes the cron has wedged (module 04). data.version is API_VERSION, SemVer for the API surface and a deploy marker (module 06). Captured live 2026-07-22.

quiz.prompt: A request for /v1/districts arrives. Where does the Worker get the payload?

options.text: It reads the dataset:v1:districts key from Workers KV.
options.feedback: Correct. Every read route fetches its KV key and wraps it in the envelope.

options.text: It recomputes the district polygons on the spot from source files.
options.feedback: No - that work happens in the cron (scheduled) rebuild, not per request. Reads are cheap KV lookups.

options.text: It queries a SQL database.
options.feedback: There is no database; the store is Workers KV.

quiz.prompt: Why does /v1/health carry Cache-Control: no-store and no ETag, while /v1/meta carries an ETag and a cacheable max-age?

options.text: Health is a live probe: no-store forces every request to origin so the reading is current, and it serves no hashed dataset body, so there is no content ETag to offer.
options.feedback: Correct. A cached health check would defeat the point, and only a hashed dataset payload earns an ETag.

options.text: Health touches no storage at all, so there is nothing to cache.
options.feedback: Not any more. Health reads the small meta record for the cron heartbeat (last_refresh_at); it just is not the kind of hashed, cacheable payload that earns an ETag.

options.text: Health is slower, so caching it would be dangerous.
options.feedback: Speed is not the reason. no-store is about always reading a live value, not about performance.

sources.label: worker/src/index.js L114-132 (health: no-store, reads meta for the heartbeat)
worker/openapi.json L14-33 (health spec: Uncached, heartbeat fields)

quiz.prompt: Which statements about the fetch() vs scheduled() split are true? Select all that apply.

options.text: fetch() handles incoming requests and reads from KV.
options.feedback: Yes - that is the read path.

options.text: scheduled() runs on a cron trigger and writes the dataset to KV.
options.feedback: Yes - that is the write path, every 5 min in prod.

options.text: The two share a cron expression so reads only work every 5 minutes.
options.feedback: No - reads are served continuously; only the rebuild is on the cron.

options.text: scheduled() answers user requests when fetch() is busy.
options.feedback: No - scheduled() is not a request handler; it is invoked by the cron, not by users.

scenario.title: FIELD RECON // TRACE ONE REQUEST
situation: > recon exercise (no incident): follow GET /v1/tags from the edge inward
> 1. request reaches the Worker fetch() handler at the Cloudflare edge
> 2. method is GET; path is looked up in the route table
> 3. handler must fetch the right KV key and wrap it in the envelope

question.prompt: Which KV key does the /v1/tags handler read?

options.text: dataset:v1:tags
options.feedback: Correct - KEYS.tags. The handler reads it and returns it inside the envelope with the shared ETag.

options.text: dataset:v1:full
options.feedback: That is the locations key (KEYS.full, the record map both location routes read), not tags.

options.text: dataset:v1:meta
options.feedback: That is the meta record; tags live under their own key.

scenario.debrief: The route table maps GET /v1/tags to a handler that reads KEYS.tags ('dataset:v1:tags') from KV and wraps it in the envelope. Same pattern for every dataset route: match path, read key, wrap. Reconnaissance complete.

recap: The API is a Cloudflare Worker; fetch() routes each request and falls through to 404.
All data is served from Workers KV under dataset:v1* keys - cheap reads, rare writes.
scheduled() rebuilds the dataset on a 5-minute cron; /v1/health is a no-store liveness + version probe that also serves the cron heartbeat (last_refresh_at / refresh_age_seconds).

modules.title: SUPPLY LINE
modules.subtitle: How fresh data gets in: the refresh job, hash-gate and last-known-good

lines: > subject: where the data comes from, and how it stays trustworthy
> [cron] sources: mods.json, tags.json, subdistricts.json, Nexus auto-discovery
> [cron] content unchanged -> no write. next tick in 5 min.

objectives: Describe how new data enters the dataset (the scheduled refresh job)
Explain the two safety rules: the hash-gate and last-known-good
Read the meta health flags: discovery_stale and generated_at

chunks.heading: The refresh job
chunks.body: The data you read is not edited live. Every 5 minutes a background job (the **cron**, from module 03) rebuilds the whole dataset: it fetches the source files (`mods.json`, `tags.json`, `subdistricts.json`) and the auto-discovered Nexus mods, merges them, and saves the result to storage (KV). The read routes you have been using just serve whatever that last rebuild produced.

chunks.heading: Two rules that keep bad data out
chunks.body: **Hash-gate**: the job computes a fingerprint (hash) of the new data and only saves it if that fingerprint changed. No change means no write, so the `dataset_version` (and your ETag) stays stable when nothing really moved. **Last-known-good**: if any source fails (Nexus is down, a file will not load), the job keeps the last good dataset, flags it, and alerts. It NEVER serves an empty or half-built dataset.

sources.label: worker/src/refresh.js L225-237 (hash-gate: write only if changed)

chunks.heading: The freshness flag: discovery_stale
chunks.body: When a refresh fails, the job sets `discovery_stale` to `true` on the meta record, keeps serving the last good data, and posts an alert. When a later refresh succeeds, it clears the flag and posts an all-clear. So `discovery_stale` in `/v1/meta` is your honest signal: `false` means the data is fresh, `true` means you are looking at the last-known-good copy while something upstream is being fixed. One honest limit: `discovery_stale` only catches a refresh that RAN and failed. If the cron stops firing altogether, no refresh runs, the flag stays `false`, and the data goes stale silently. That case is what the `/v1/health` cron heartbeat from module 03 exists to catch, so read the two together: `discovery_stale` for a failed refresh, `refresh_age_seconds` for a missing one.

sources.label: worker/src/index.js L114-132 (heartbeat catches the missing-refresh case discovery_stale cannot)

chunks.heading: Why a half-page is worse than no page
chunks.body: Auto-discovery reads the Nexus results one page at a time. If a page fails partway through, the code **throws an error** instead of using what it managed to get. Why so strict? Because a truncated list looks exactly like a complete one: 40 mods when there are really 55 would silently drop 15 pins from the map, with no error anywhere. Throwing turns a silent data loss into a visible failure, which then triggers last-known-good. A partial answer you cannot tell apart from a full one is worse than a clean failure.

sources.label: worker/src/nexus.js L45-93 (throw on any page failure)

lab.title: STATUS BOARD
lab.briefing: Read the dataset's own health record. /v1/meta reports whether it is fresh or serving last-known-good (discovery_stale) and which tagged mods were skipped; the envelope's generated_at says when it was last built.
steps: TRANSMIT and read data.discovery_stale and generated_at (how old is the build?)
Switch to the 'stale' case and watch the flag flip to true
Switch to the 'not ready' case: a 503 returned before the first build exists

shapeHints: a 503 not_ready has no dataset yet and carries Retry-After

lab.debrief: The healthy case is a real capture. The stale case (discovery_stale=true, an older generated_at) and the 503 not_ready case are reconstructed exactly from the code, because they cannot be captured safely while the API is healthy. Note the 503 carries Retry-After: 60 and no dataset yet; it means 'built nothing so far, try again shortly', which is different from the 429 you will meet in module 05.

quiz.prompt: You fetch /v1/meta and see discovery_stale: true. What does it mean?

options.text: The last refresh failed, so you are being served the last-known-good data while the problem is fixed.
options.feedback: Correct. The data is still valid, just not freshly rebuilt; a later successful refresh clears the flag.

options.text: The API is down and returning errors.
options.feedback: No. The API is up and serving data; the flag only says the most recent rebuild did not succeed.

options.text: Your cached copy is stale and you must send If-None-Match.
options.feedback: That is a caching concern (module 02). discovery_stale is about the server-side rebuild, not your cache.

quiz.prompt: Put one refresh cycle in order.
steps: Fetch the source files and the Nexus auto-discovery data
Build the merged dataset in memory
Compute the content fingerprint (hash) of the new dataset
Compare it to the stored dataset_version
Write to KV only if the fingerprint changed; on any source failure, keep last-known-good and flag discovery_stale

scenario.title: INCIDENT LOG // THE TRUNCATED TAG
situation: > [cron] auto-discovery paging Nexus: page 1 ok (50), page 2 ...
> [nexus] page 2 request returned HTTP 500 partway through
> decision point: the job already has 50 of ~60 mods in hand
> option A: keep the 50 and rebuild. option B: throw and abort.

question.prompt: The second Nexus page failed. What should the refresh do with the 50 mods it already has?

options.text: Throw and abort the rebuild; keep serving the last-known-good dataset and flag discovery_stale.
options.feedback: Correct. A partial list is indistinguishable from a complete one, so using it would silently drop ~10 mods from the map. Throwing preserves the last good data and surfaces the failure.

options.text: Rebuild with the 50 it has; a mostly-complete map is better than a stale one.
options.feedback: This is the trap. Nobody can tell 50-of-60 from a real 50, so the missing mods vanish with no error. Freshness is not worth silent data loss.

options.text: Retry forever until the page succeeds.
options.feedback: Blocking the whole cycle on one flaky page risks never finishing. Fail fast, keep last-known-good, and let the next 5-minute tick try again.

scenario.debrief: What the code does: the Nexus fetch throws on any page failure, so a truncated tag population can never be mistaken for the full set. That error triggers last-known-good and discovery_stale, and the next cron tick retries. The rule: treat a partial fetch as a failure, never as a smaller success.

recap: A background job rebuilds the dataset every 5 minutes and saves it to KV.
Hash-gate: write only when the content fingerprint changes. Last-known-good: on any source failure, keep the last good data and flag discovery_stale, never serve empty.
A partial Nexus page is treated as a failure, not a smaller success, so mods are never silently dropped.

modules.title: PERIMETER DEFENSE
modules.subtitle: Rate limiting and bot protection at the edge

lines: > subject: the wall in front of the API
> [edge] ...101 requests from one address inside 10 seconds...

objectives: Explain what the rate limit does and where it runs (the edge, before the Worker)
Read a 429 as 'slow down', not 'the API is broken'
Recognize why an automated or datacenter client can trip edge defenses

chunks.heading: The wall at the edge
chunks.body: Before a request ever reaches the Worker, it passes through Cloudflare's **WAF** (Web Application Firewall, the security layer at the edge). One rule there is a **rate limit**: for any path starting with `/v1/`, if a single IP address sends more than **100 requests in 10 seconds**, further requests are **blocked** for 10 seconds. This shields the API from a runaway client or a flood, and it costs the Worker nothing because the edge handles it before any code runs.

sources.label: Cloudflare dashboard (edge config, NOT in the repo): rule 'API rate limit', path starts_with /v1/, 100 req / 10s per IP, action Block, 10s timeout, Free plan - confirmed 2026-07-06

chunks.heading: 429 is not an outage
chunks.body: A blocked client gets **HTTP 429 Too Many Requests** straight from Cloudflare's edge. Two things matter. First, 429 means 'you sent too many requests, slow down and retry later', not 'the API is broken'. Second, because the block happens at the edge, **the Worker never runs**, so a 429 does NOT arrive in our JSON envelope: it is Cloudflare's own default response (on the Free plan you cannot customize it). A bare 429 with no envelope is the edge talking, not the API.

sources.label: Cloudflare - Rate limiting rules (Block returns 429, error 1015)

chunks.heading: Bot Fight Mode, and why it bit us
chunks.body: Cloudflare also offers **Bot Fight Mode** (BFM), a free feature that, in Cloudflare's words, 'helps detect and mitigate bot traffic' by throwing computationally expensive challenges at requests it judges to be automated. That sounds harmless, but this project's own health monitor runs from GitHub Actions, a datacenter that BFM treats as suspicious. BFM challenged it and returned failures that looked like an outage. The fix was to turn Bot Fight Mode OFF for the whole zone: the targeted `/v1/` rate limit is the right control for a public, read-only API that is meant to be called by machines.

sources.label: Cloudflare dashboard (edge config, NOT in the repo): Bot Fight Mode = Off, Free plan - confirmed 2026-07-06

chunks.heading: Reading an edge block
chunks.body: A diagnosis rule from that incident: if BOTH production and staging fail at once, with an edge-level status code (a 403 from a challenge, a 429 from the rate limit) and no JSON envelope, the problem is almost certainly Cloudflare configuration, not the Worker code. One environment failing points at your code; both at once, at the shared edge in front of them.

sources.label: worker/.github monitor rationale: in-game mods have no fallback, so a silent edge block must page (monitor-api-health.yml L1-6)

lab.title: PROBING THE PERIMETER
lab.briefing: A careful probe. In simulation this shows a normal 200 and, separately, the canned 429 you would get if you crossed the limit. We never approach 100 requests in 10 seconds: the rate limit is there to be respected, not tested.
steps: TRANSMIT for a normal 200 from the API
Switch to the 'rate limited' case to see the edge 429
Notice the 429 has no JSON envelope: it is the edge, not the Worker

shapeHints: a 429 is Cloudflare's edge response, not the JSON envelope

lab.debrief: The healthy 200 is a real capture. The 429 is reconstructed from Cloudflare's documented behavior (error 1015, a plain edge response with Retry-After), because we deliberately never trigger the real limit. The takeaway: a rate-limited caller is stopped at the edge and gets no envelope. Respect the 100/10s budget and space your requests.

quiz.prompt: Your client gets an HTTP 429 from /v1/locations. What is the right response?

options.text: Slow down: wait (honor Retry-After) and retry at a lower rate. It is rate limiting, not an outage.
options.feedback: Correct. 429 means you exceeded the request budget; back off and try again shortly.

options.text: Treat the API as down and alert the maintainer.
options.feedback: No. The API is fine; your client is sending too fast. Fix the client's request rate.

options.text: Retry immediately in a tight loop until it works.
options.feedback: That makes it worse: hammering an address that is being rate limited just extends the block.

quiz.prompt: Which statement about the perimeter is WRONG?

options.text: "The /v1/ rate limit is enforced inside the Worker's own code."
options.feedback: This is the wrong one. The rate limit is a Cloudflare WAF rule at the edge; it blocks before the Worker runs, which is why a 429 has no envelope.

options.text: "A rate-limited request returns 429 without the JSON envelope."
options.feedback: True. The edge blocks it before the Worker can wrap anything.

options.text: "Bot Fight Mode can challenge automated clients like a CI runner."
options.feedback: True, and that is exactly what caused the false outage.

options.text: "The limit counts requests per IP address over a 10-second window."
options.feedback: True: 100 requests per 10 seconds per IP on /v1/ paths.

scenario.title: INCIDENT LOG // THE PHANTOM OUTAGE
situation: > [monitor] health check: https://api.nczoning.net -> HTTP 403
> [monitor] health check: https://api-dev.nczoning.net -> HTTP 403
> pager: 'DATA API DOWN (both environments)'
> but: real users are loading the map fine right now

question.prompt: Both environments return 403 to the monitor, yet users are fine. Is the API down?

options.text: No. Both environments failing at once with an edge status, while users are fine, points at Cloudflare: Bot Fight Mode is challenging the datacenter monitor. Check edge config.
options.feedback: Correct, and this is what happened. BFM challenged the GitHub Actions runner; disabling BFM fixed it. The Worker was never down.

options.text: Yes. Redeploy the Worker to both environments.
options.feedback: A redeploy fixes nothing here: the code is fine and serving users. The block is at the edge, before the Worker.

options.text: Yes. The KV data is corrupted in both environments.
options.feedback: Corrupt data would not return a 403 from the edge, and would not spare real users. The status code and 'both at once' both point at Cloudflare.

scenario.debrief: What actually happened: Bot Fight Mode was on and challenged the GitHub Actions health monitor (a datacenter IP), so both environments failed the check at once with an edge 403, while the API served users normally. Disabling BFM fixed it. The rule: both environments failing with an edge status code points at Cloudflare configuration, not your code. (Sourced from the incident record; this lives in Cloudflare edge config, not the repo.)

recap: A Cloudflare WAF rate limit (100 requests / 10s per IP on /v1/) blocks floods at the edge, before the Worker runs.
A rate-limited client gets a bare 429 (no envelope) because the edge stops it; 429 means slow down, not outage.
Bot Fight Mode challenges automated clients and once caused a false outage; both-environments-at-once with an edge status means look at Cloudflare, not the code.

modules.title: LAUNCH AUTHORIZATION
modules.subtitle: Shipping the API: CI/CD and Cloudflare tokens

lines: > subject: how a git push becomes a live API

objectives: Describe what happens when code is pushed to main (test gate, then deploy)
Explain how the deploy proves who it is to Cloudflare (a scoped token in a secret)
Use the /v1/health version as a deploy marker

chunks.heading: From push to production
chunks.body: When code is pushed to the `main` branch, a **CI/CD** pipeline runs automatically. CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) means the project builds, tests, and ships itself without anyone running deploy commands by hand. Here the pipeline is **GitHub Actions** (GitHub's built-in automation). On a push that touches `worker/`, it installs dependencies, runs the test suite as a **gate**, and only if every test passes does it deploy the Worker: production from `main`, staging from `dev`.

chunks.heading: The key to the door: a scoped API token
chunks.body: To deploy, the pipeline must prove to Cloudflare that it is allowed to. It does this with a **Cloudflare API token**: a credential scoped to only the permissions it needs (this is 'least privilege', giving out no more access than required). The token is stored as a **GitHub Actions secret** (`CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN`), which GitHub keeps encrypted and hides from build logs, so it never appears in the code or the output.

sources.label: GitHub Docs - Using secrets in GitHub Actions
Cloudflare - Create an API token (scoped permissions)

chunks.heading: Two ways a token fails
chunks.body: Getting the token wrong is the classic deploy blocker, and it bit this project twice. (1) A token missing a required permission is rejected by Cloudflare with **error code 10000** (an authentication error); the fix is to widen the token's scope to include what the deploy actually needs. (2) A scoped token could not look up which account to deploy to; the fix was to set **`account_id`** directly in `wrangler.jsonc` so the deploy tool skips the account-discovery step (which would otherwise need an extra permission). Account IDs are not secret, so they live in the committed config.

sources.label: worker/wrangler.jsonc L18-21 (account_id set so CI's scoped token skips the account lookup)

chunks.heading: The deploy marker
chunks.body: How do you know a deploy actually landed? `API_VERSION` is set by hand in `wrangler.jsonc`, and the Worker reports it at `/v1/health` as `data.version`. Read it right after a deploy: if it is the value you shipped, your code is serving; if it is the old one, the deploy did not take. It is SemVer for the API SURFACE, not the site's release version: MINOR when a field or route is added, MAJOR on a breaking change (which also moves `/v1` to `/v2`), PATCH on a behavior fix. That makes it two tools in one: the cheapest possible deploy smoke test, and the signal a consumer reads to tell whether the field it wants exists yet. Worth knowing why it works that way: the marker sat at `0.1.0` through three additive changes because nothing forced a bump, so CI now fails any change to the API's shape that forgets to move it.

sources.label: worker/test/api-version.test.js L33-47 (CI fails a shape change with no version bump)

chunks.heading: The deploy can be triggered by a robot, too
chunks.body: A push is not the only way to deploy. `deploy-api.yml` also accepts a `workflow_dispatch` (a run triggered by hand or by another workflow), added so the health monitor can heal a wedged cron on its own. When the monitor sees `refresh_age_seconds` climbing past the limit (module 03), it dispatches this same workflow to redeploy the Worker, which re-registers the cron trigger and revives the 5-minute refresh. Because the dispatch runs the FULL pipeline (test gate included) and the ref still selects the environment, the self-heal ships the correct, tested code, never a bare `wrangler deploy` fired from the monitor. A budget cap (a couple of redeploys per environment per hour) stops it looping; past that it escalates to a human.

lab.title: CONFIRM THE DEPLOY
lab.briefing: After a deploy, the fastest check is the version marker. Read data.version from /v1/health: that is the API_VERSION that is actually live.
steps: TRANSMIT the health request
Read data.version: this is the deployed API_VERSION
After a release you would compare it to the version you just shipped
lab.debrief: data.version is 1.3.0, the current API_VERSION. Because the deploy only runs after the test gate passes, seeing the expected version here means tests passed AND the deploy landed. A version other than the one you expected means the deploy did not take, time to check the pipeline. The 1.3.0 reads as three additive changes on top of the original /v1 surface: recently_updated, then archives, then the cron heartbeat.

quiz.prompt: A push to main touches worker/. What must happen before it deploys to production?

options.text: The test suite must pass; it is a gate before the deploy step.
options.feedback: Correct. The workflow runs npm test first and only deploys if it passes, so a failing test blocks a bad release.

options.text: A maintainer must click 'approve deploy'.
options.feedback: No manual approval step here; the gate is automated (the tests).

options.text: Nothing; every push deploys immediately.
options.feedback: No. The tests run first, and a path filter means only worker/ changes trigger the API deploy at all.

quiz.prompt: Which statement is WRONG?

options.text: "account_id must be kept secret, like the API token."
options.feedback: This is the wrong one. Account IDs are not secret; account_id is set in the committed wrangler.jsonc precisely so the scoped token does not need account-lookup permission. The API token is the secret.

options.text: "The Cloudflare API token is stored as an encrypted GitHub Actions secret."
options.feedback: True: it is referenced as secrets.CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN and never printed.

options.text: "Cloudflare error 10000 is an authentication problem with the token."
options.feedback: True: it means the token cannot do what the deploy asked; widen its scope.

options.text: "/v1/health reports the deployed API_VERSION."
options.feedback: True: that is what makes it a deploy marker.

scenario.title: INCIDENT LOG // CODE 10000
situation: > [ci] wrangler deploy (production)
> [ci] error: Authentication error [code: 10000]
> deploy failed; production still on the previous version
> the code is fine; the tests passed

question.prompt: The deploy fails with Cloudflare error 10000. What is wrong, and how do you fix it?

options.text: The API token cannot do what the deploy needs (a missing permission, or it cannot discover the account). Widen the token's scope and set account_id in wrangler.jsonc.
options.feedback: Correct. Error 10000 is Cloudflare for 'this token is not authorized for that'. Scoping the token correctly and setting account_id (so it skips the account lookup) is exactly the fix this project used.

options.text: The application code is broken; revert the last commit.
options.feedback: The tests passed and the code compiled; 10000 is an auth error at deploy time, not a code bug.

options.text: Cloudflare is down; wait and retry.
options.feedback: A specific auth error code is not an outage. It is telling you the token lacks permission.

scenario.debrief: What actually happened: the deploy's API token was not scoped for everything wrangler needed, and separately could not discover the account. Error 10000 is Cloudflare's 'this token cannot do that'. The fixes: give the token the right permissions, and set account_id in wrangler.jsonc so it does not need the account-lookup permission at all. Both are visible in the config below.

recap: A push to main runs GitHub Actions: install, test as a gate, then deploy the Worker (staging from dev).
The deploy authenticates with a scoped Cloudflare API token kept as an encrypted GitHub Actions secret.
account_id is set in wrangler.jsonc (not secret) so the token skips account lookup; /v1/health version is the deploy marker.

modules.title: THE SPEC IS THE LAW
modules.subtitle: OpenAPI, browsable docs, and guarding against drift

lines: > subject: the written contract, and the guard that keeps it honest

objectives: Explain what the OpenAPI spec is and where it lives
Describe how the drift-guard test keeps the spec and the code in agreement
Read a single path out of the spec

chunks.heading: The contract, written down
chunks.body: Everything modules 01-06 taught (the endpoints, the envelope, the ETags) is also written down in one machine-readable file: the **OpenAPI** description at `/openapi.json`. **OpenAPI** is a standard, language-neutral way to describe an HTTP API so that both people and tools can understand it without reading the source code. It lists every path, its parameters (like `?full=1`), and the shape of each response.

chunks.heading: Human docs for free
chunks.body: A machine-readable file is not much fun to read, so the API's root page (`GET /`) renders that same spec with **Scalar**, a documentation renderer, into browsable, interactive docs. A modder who visits `api.nczoning.net` lands there. The important part: the spec file is the single source of truth, and the docs page just displays it. Write the spec once, get the docs automatically.

sources.label: worker/src/docs.js L1-29 (root page renders openapi.json with Scalar)

chunks.heading: The spec must not lie: the drift guard
chunks.body: A written contract is only useful if it stays true. A test enforces that in BOTH directions: every route the Worker actually serves must appear in the spec (no undocumented routes), and every `/v1` path in the spec must actually be served (no stale, removed paths). If the code and the spec ever disagree, the test fails in CI and the change cannot merge. The docs can never quietly fall behind the code.

chunks.heading: Reading a path from the spec

lab.title: CROSS-CHECK THE SPEC
lab.briefing: Fetch the machine-readable spec and confirm it agrees with what you learned: the paths it lists, and that /v1/locations documents the full no-op alias plus the 304 and 503 responses.
steps: TRANSMIT and read the list of paths under `paths`
Find /v1/locations and check its `full` parameter (documented as a no-op alias) and its 200/304/503 responses
Confirm the paths match the six routes you have been using

shapeHints: paths lists the six /v1 routes plus / and /openapi.json's routes

lab.debrief: Trimmed here for readability; the real /openapi.json carries full request and response schemas for each path. The point is that this file, not a hand-written doc, is the contract, and the drift-guard test keeps it matched to the code.

quiz.prompt: What does the drift-guard test guarantee?

options.text: The routes the Worker serves and the paths documented in the spec match each other, both ways.
options.feedback: Correct. It fails if a served route is undocumented, or if a documented /v1 path is not served.

options.text: That the API responds within a time limit.
options.feedback: No, that would be a performance test. The drift guard is about spec-vs-code agreement.

options.text: That the spec is valid JSON.
options.feedback: It does check the spec is well-formed, but its real job is catching drift between the spec and the actual routes.

quiz.prompt: Where is the single source of truth for the API's documentation?

options.text: worker/openapi.json; the root page just renders it with Scalar.
options.feedback: Correct. Write the spec once and the browsable docs come for free.

options.text: A hand-maintained README that must be updated separately.
options.feedback: No. A separate doc would be exactly the thing that drifts; the spec is the source, checked by the drift guard.

options.text: The Scalar library.
options.feedback: Scalar only renders the spec; it is not the source of the content.

scenario.title: INCIDENT LOG // SPEC DRIFT
situation: > a contributor adds a new route to the Worker: GET /v1/regions
> they wire it up, it works locally, they open a pull request
> [ci] running worker tests...
> [ci] FAIL: route /v1/regions is served but missing from openapi.json

question.prompt: CI is red on the new route. What is the test telling them, and what is the fix?

options.text: The drift guard caught an undocumented route. Add /v1/regions to openapi.json (or remove the route) so the spec and the code agree.
options.feedback: Correct. The spec is the contract; a served-but-undocumented route is exactly the drift the test exists to block.

options.text: The route has a bug; rewrite the handler.
options.feedback: The handler works. The failure is about the spec not mentioning the route, not about the route's behavior.

options.text: Delete the test; it is blocking a working feature.
options.feedback: The test is doing its job. Deleting it would let the docs silently fall out of sync with the API.

scenario.debrief: What the test enforces: the spec and the router must list the same routes. A new route with no spec entry fails 'served but missing from openapi.json'; a spec entry with no route fails 'documented but not served'. Either way the contributor updates the spec, and the docs stay honest. (Constructed scenario, but the two-way check is exactly what the test does.)

recap: The OpenAPI spec at /openapi.json is the machine-readable contract for the whole API.
The root page renders that same spec with Scalar, so the docs are always generated from the source of truth.
A CI test guards against drift in both directions: served routes must be documented, documented paths must be served.

modules.title: FIRST CUSTOMER
modules.subtitle: Who consumes the API, and the patterns that keep them safe

lines: > subject: the API has customers. meet them.

objectives: Name the API's two consumers, and how the in-game one stays version-safe and thread-safe
Explain the API-primary-with-fallback pattern
Recognize the composite-UID pitfall that silently drops images

chunks.heading: Who actually calls this API
chunks.body: A **consumer** is any program that calls the API. This one has two, and both are open source. The **website** reads `/v1/locations` in the browser; since the B7 change it does no Nexus merge of its own, because the server already merged everything. The **in-game mod** (NCZoningCore) fetches the same route once per game launch and hands the data to other Cyberpunk mods through a small redscript contract. Different languages, same endpoint, same records. (Both still send `?full=1` in the URL, a habit from the old two-shape API - today it is a documented no-op alias, which is exactly why the alias exists.)

sources.label: assets/js/services.js L19-98 (website reads the Data API)
nc-zoning-core NCZoningFetcher.reds L1-9 (in-game mod fetches /v1/locations?full=1 once per launch)

chunks.heading: The in-game consumer: safe by design
chunks.body: The in-game mod does two hard things so the mods that depend on it do not have to. **Threading**: it parses the network response off the game thread, then marshals the result back onto the game thread before it updates anything or fires an event, so every value a dependent mod sees is already safe to touch. **The ApiVersion handshake**: redscript cannot check a dependency's version at all, so the mod exposes `ApiVersion()`, an integer that only ever increments on a breaking change. A dependent mod gates on it (`if ApiVersion() < 1 { return; }`) and then listens for three events: `DataReady`, `DataRefreshed`, and `DataError`. It is the same idea as the API's own `/v1` path versioning, one link further down the chain. And the DTO tracks the API: when the record gained `recently_updated`, NCZLocation gained the matching field and a `RecentlyUpdated()` accessor - an *additive* change, so `ApiVersion()` stayed at 1.

sources.label: nc-zoning-core NCZLocation.reds L41, L61-63 (recently_updated field + accessor)

chunks.heading: A capability built for the in-game consumer: archives
chunks.body: The `archives` field on every record (module 01) exists for exactly this customer. It lists the `.archive` and `.xl` files a mod ships, all of which land in the game's `archive/pc/mod/` folder. Only a consumer running inside the game can read that folder, so only the in-game mod can match those filenames and tell a player which location mods they already have installed. The server does the hard part (resolving each mod's files from Nexus), so the list arrives ready to use. And it is an additive field: it appeared without touching `/v1` or `ApiVersion()`, so when the in-game DTO adds it the handshake stays at 1, the same additive pattern this module keeps returning to.

sources.label: worker/src/nexus.js L178-221 (archive detection built for in-game install matching)

chunks.heading: API-primary, and deliberately no fallback
chunks.body: The website tries the API, and ONLY the API. If anything goes wrong (a network error, a non-2xx status, a malformed body, or a payload missing `description` - the signature of an older deploy still serving the pre-1.4.0 slim shape), the code **throws**, and the site shows a loud 'map data unavailable' state. There is **no client-side fallback**: an earlier version fell back to fetching Nexus directly in the browser, but that path was removed on purpose. Why give up a safety net? Because the API's other consumer, in-game mods, has no fallback either, so a silent browser workaround would mask an outage that still breaks them. Failing loudly keeps the public site a **canary**: if the map is down, the API is down, and someone finds out.

sources.label: assets/js/services.js L8-18 (the only data path; deliberately no fallback)
assets/js/services.js L44-65 (throws on any failure; slim-payload guard)

chunks.heading: The consumer caches too (304 in the wild)
chunks.body: Remember the conditional GET from module 02? Here it is from the client side. The website stores the last ETag and body in the browser's `localStorage` and sends `If-None-Match` on the next load. If the data has not changed it gets a **304** and reuses the cached copy: no re-download of 296 entries. The server issues the ETag; the consumer spends it. Same mechanism, both ends.

chunks.heading: The silent-drop trap: composite UIDs
chunks.body: To fetch a mod's thumbnail, Nexus wants a **composite UID**: the game id and mod id packed into one number as `(gameId << 32) + modId`, NOT the string `"gameId:modId"`. Get the format wrong and Nexus does not return an error; it simply returns no images. The server and the website must compute this identically, or thumbnails vanish with nothing in the logs to explain it.

sources.label: worker/src/nexus.js L95-103 (composite UID must match the site's, or Nexus silently drops it)

lab.title: CONSUME LIKE THE MAP DOES
lab.briefing: Fetch the list the way the website does, and read the first entry end to end: identity, placement, story, imagery, the server-computed recently_updated flag, and the archives install list.
steps: TRANSMIT the full request (?full=1 is already set)
Read data[0]: note description, credits, thumbnail_url, picture_url, archives
This is exactly the shape services.js maps into the app

shapeHints: one ETag, shared with the plain /v1/locations request
entries carry description, credits, image URLs, recently_updated and archives

data.name: 402 north oak - brutalist apartment house home
data.description: brutalist home in the north oak hills with a view of the city from the flowing infinite pool

lab.debrief: This is the exact payload the website consumes (real capture, truncated to the first of 296 entries). services.js reads envelope.data, maps source/image fields into the app's internal shape, and caches the body against its ETag for next time. A consumer only needs the envelope plus the documented fields; everything else it does is defensive.

sources.label: assets/js/services.js L75-95 (map API entries to internal shape)

quiz.prompt: The Data API call fails. The site throws and shows a 'map data unavailable' state instead of silently rendering an empty map. Why is failing loudly the right choice here?

options.text: The API's other consumer (in-game mods) has no fallback, so a silent browser workaround would hide a real outage. A loud failure keeps the public site a canary.
options.feedback: Correct. There is deliberately no client-side fallback; the visible error is the signal that the API needs attention.

options.text: So the site can fall back to fetching Nexus directly in the browser.
options.feedback: Not any more. That legacy fallback was removed on purpose; the site now surfaces the error instead of quietly working around it.

options.text: To crash the page so the user reloads.
options.feedback: It is not a crash. The failure is caught and rendered as a clear 'data unavailable' state, not a broken page.

quiz.prompt: Which statement about consuming this API is WRONG?

options.text: "To get thumbnails, send Nexus the UID as the string 'gameId:modId'."
options.feedback: This is the wrong one. Nexus needs the packed number (gameId << 32) + modId; the string form makes it silently return no images.

options.text: "The website caches the response and sends If-None-Match to get a 304."
options.feedback: True: it stores the ETag and body in localStorage and revalidates conditionally.

options.text: "If the API returns a slim body when full was requested, the site rejects it and surfaces the error state."
options.feedback: True: a missing description means the payload is unusable for popups, so the site throws rather than ship a degraded map.

options.text: "A consumer reads the payload from envelope.data."
options.feedback: True: the data always lives under the envelope's data key.

scenario.title: INCIDENT LOG // THE VANISHING THUMBNAILS
situation: > after a change to the image-fetch code, mod thumbnails stop appearing
> the map loads, pins are correct, data is fresh (discovery_stale: false)
> Nexus returns 200 OK for the image query, but the node list is empty
> no errors in any log

question.prompt: Images are gone but nothing errors. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The composite UID is being built in the wrong format, so Nexus matches nothing and returns an empty (but successful) result.
options.feedback: Correct. A 200 with an empty node list is the classic silent-drop signature: the request was well-formed but the UIDs did not match Nexus's packed-integer format.

options.text: The API is down.
options.feedback: No: the map, pins, and freshness are all fine. Only images are affected, which points at the image-UID path specifically.

options.text: The rate limit is blocking the image requests.
options.feedback: A rate limit would return 429, not a 200 with empty data. The success status rules it out.

scenario.debrief: What actually happened: the composite UID was built in the wrong format, so Nexus returned 200 with no matching nodes, and the images silently dropped. The fix was to compute the UID exactly as (gameId << 32) + modId, matching the server and site. The rule: when an upstream returns success but empty, suspect your request format, not an outage. (Grounded in the composite-UID invariant in the code below.)

recap: Two consumers, one representation: the in-game mod and the website read the same /v1/locations records (since B7 the browser does no Nexus merge; ?full=1 survives as a no-op alias).
API-primary, no fallback: the site throws on any failure and shows a loud 'map data unavailable' state, so it stays a canary for API outages (in-game mods have no fallback either).
It caches with ETag + If-None-Match (304 from the client side); and a wrong composite-UID format makes Nexus silently drop images.

modules.title: FIELD CERTIFICATION
modules.subtitle: Capstone: a mixed-signal diagnostic across the whole API

lines: > NC-ACAD-01 // clearance check: LEVEL 9 // CERTIFICATION EXAM
> no new material. this is the field test.
> you will read live signals and decide what, if anything, is wrong.
> pass, and the Division issues a CERTIFIED stamp. begin.

objectives: Diagnose a mixed-signal situation using signals from across the API
Tell a real problem apart from the system working as designed
Choose the correct action (or correctly choose no action)

chunks.heading: The mission
chunks.body: Everything you need you already learned. A good operator reads several signals together: the health probe (`/v1/health`, including `refresh_age_seconds`), the dataset's own report (`/v1/meta`, especially `discovery_stale` and `generated_at`), the status codes that come back, and whether real users are affected. Many 'incidents' are the system behaving exactly as designed. Your job in the field is to know the difference, and to not 'fix' things that are not broken.

lab.title: FULL DIAGNOSTIC SWEEP
lab.briefing: Read the dataset's own report. In the field you would cross-check this against /v1/health and the status codes you are seeing.
steps: TRANSMIT and read discovery_stale (fresh or last-known-good?)
Read generated_at (how old is the build?) and scan data.skipped
Decide: is anything actually wrong here?

shapeHints: skipped lists tagged mods whose metadata block did not parse - want a count? group the /v1/locations records

lab.debrief: A healthy board: discovery_stale is false (fresh), the build is recent, and one skipped entry is a note for that mod's author, not an incident. Nothing to do. Half of operations is confidently recognizing 'all green' and not inventing work.

quiz.prompt: A consumer wants to detect when the dataset changed, as cheaply as possible. What should it do?

options.text: Store the ETag and send If-None-Match; a 304 means unchanged, a 200 with a new ETag means it changed.
options.feedback: Correct. That is the conditional GET; the ETag is the dataset_version, so it changes only when the data does.

options.text: Download the full list every minute and diff it.
options.feedback: Wasteful. The whole point of the ETag/304 mechanism is to avoid re-downloading unchanged data.

options.text: Poll /v1/health and watch the version.
options.feedback: version is API_VERSION: it moves when the API's SHAPE changes (a new field or route), not when the data does. Data changes are tracked by dataset_version / ETag.

quiz.prompt: A client receives HTTP 429 with an HTML body and no envelope. What happened, and what should it do?

options.text: It hit the edge rate limit (100/10s per IP). The Worker never ran, so there is no envelope. Slow down and retry.
options.feedback: Correct. A bare 429 is Cloudflare's edge talking; back off and respect the budget.

options.text: The dataset is corrupt; report an outage.
options.feedback: 429 is rate limiting, not data corruption, and the absence of an envelope points at the edge, not the Worker.

options.text: The API changed its response format.
options.feedback: The API did not respond at all; the edge blocked the request before the Worker could wrap anything.

quiz.prompt: A deploy just merged to main. Put the confirmation steps in order.
steps: CI runs the worker tests as a gate
On green, wrangler deploys the Worker to production
You GET /v1/health and read data.version
You confirm data.version matches the release you shipped

quiz.prompt: GET /v1/meta shows discovery_stale: false and users are fine, but GET /v1/health shows refresh_age_seconds climbing past 30 minutes (the cron runs every 5). What is happening?

options.text: The refresh cron has wedged: it stopped firing, so no refresh runs to fail, discovery_stale never trips, and the API is silently serving stale data. The heartbeat is the only signal that catches this.
options.feedback: Correct. discovery_stale only flags a refresh that RAN and failed; a cron that stopped needs the heartbeat. The monitor pages on this and can self-heal by redeploying (module 06).

options.text: Nothing: discovery_stale is false, so the data is fresh by definition.
options.feedback: That is the trap. discovery_stale false only means no refresh FAILED. A cron that never ran cannot fail, so the flag stays false while the data ages. refresh_age_seconds is what reveals it.

options.text: The edge is rate limiting the cron.
options.feedback: The cron is a scheduled Worker invocation, not an inbound /v1 request, so the IP rate limit does not apply to it. A climbing heartbeat points at the cron not firing, not the edge.

sources.label: worker/src/index.js L114-132 (refresh_age_seconds); worker/src/refresh.js L253 (heartbeat stamped every cycle)

scenario.title: FIELD CERTIFICATION // MIXED SIGNAL
situation: > reports: 'the map data looks a little behind'
> GET /v1/meta -> 200, discovery_stale: true, generated_at is ~30 min ago
> GET /v1/health -> 200, version 1.3.0 (the expected release)
> users are loading the map fine; pins are present

question.prompt: Certification question: what is happening, and what is the right action?

options.text: A refresh failed, so the API is serving last-known-good (discovery_stale) while otherwise healthy. No emergency action; it self-recovers on the next successful cron. Monitor, and check the upstream (Nexus) if it persists.
options.feedback: Correct. Health is fine, users are fine, data is just not freshly rebuilt. This is the last-known-good design working. The all-clear fires automatically when a refresh next succeeds.

options.text: The deploy is broken; roll back the Worker.
options.feedback: health returns the expected version and users are fine; this is not a deploy problem. discovery_stale is about the data refresh, not the code.

options.text: The edge is rate limiting everyone; disable the WAF rule.
options.feedback: Nobody is getting 429s and users are fine. Disabling a security control to chase an unrelated symptom would be the wrong move.

scenario.debrief: What the signals say together: discovery_stale true + an older generated_at means a refresh failed and last-known-good is being served; health 200 with the right version means the code and deploy are fine; happy users confirm no outage. The correct action is usually none: the next successful cron clears the flag and posts the all-clear. Certification is knowing that 'stale but healthy' is a designed state, not an incident. Field test complete.

recap: Read signals together: /v1/health (is the code up, and is the cron still firing? watch refresh_age_seconds), /v1/meta discovery_stale + generated_at (did a refresh fail?), and the status codes (envelope = Worker, bare 429 = edge).
Many 'incidents' are the system as designed: last-known-good, cache windows, and rate limits are features, not faults.
The best action is often no action. CERTIFIED.
